summary:
The Magellan-Elcano circumnavigation (1519–1522 AD) was the first voyage to sail completely around the Earth, demonstrating beyond doubt the planet's spherical nature and its vast oceanic extent, and completing the geographic framework within which all subsequent exploration would be conducted. Ferdinand Magellan, a Portuguese navigator sailing for Spain, departed Seville in September 1519 with five ships and 270 men. The expedition's stated mission was to reach the Spice Islands (Maluku/Moluccas) by sailing west — completing the circumnavigation by accident as a side effect of finding a westward route to the East. Magellan discovered the strait bearing his name at the tip of South America (navigated in the tempestuous winter of 1520), crossed the vast Pacific (which he named for its apparent calm), and reached the Philippines in 1521 — where he was killed in the Battle of Mactan by the chieftain Lapu-Lapu.

Juan Sebastián Elcano, a Spanish navigator who had participated in a mutiny earlier in the voyage (and was pardoned), took command of the surviving vessel Victoria and completed the circumnavigation, arriving back in Spain in September 1522 with just 18 of the original 270 crew members. The voyage's scientific and geographic revelations were epoch-making: the Pacific was far larger than any European had imagined (the crew nearly starved crossing it), South America extended much further south than believed, and the Earth's circumference was confirmed. Practically, the voyage opened direct Spanish contact with the Philippines — eventually leading to the [[event:2426|Manila Galleon]] trade — and established that the world was a single interconnected ocean system. Pigafetta's journal of the voyage, one of the great travel narratives, became a foundation of European geographic knowledge.
